title: Flex axes and sizing

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling draft for Flex axes and sizing; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CSS Snapshot 2024; W3C Working Group Note 2024; provisions applicable to Flex axes and sizing
sources.claim: Flex layout distributes items along a main axis and aligns them along a cross axis chosen by flex-direction and writing mode.

sources.locator: MDN CSS Guide; accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Flex axes and sizing
sources.claim: Flex layout distributes items along a main axis and aligns them along a cross axis chosen by flex-direction and writing mode.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Flex layout distributes items along a main axis and aligns them along a cross axis chosen by flex-direction and writing mode.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A column flex container uses justify-content for vertical distribution and align-items for horizontal cross-axis alignment. Your goal is to implement and explain a resilient one-dimensional component layout. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Normal flow and display; Intrinsic sizing and constraints. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Flex layout distributes items along a main axis and aligns them along a cross axis chosen by flex-direction and writing mode. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Row is not always physical horizontal, wrapping creates multiple lines, and order changes visual placement without changing DOM order. This draft therefore rejects: justify-content always controls horizontal alignment.

includes: Use flex containers, direction, basis, growth, shrinkage and wrapping deliberately.
Implement and explain a resilient one-dimensional component layout.
excludes: Row is not always physical horizontal, wrapping creates multiple lines, and order changes visual placement without changing DOM order.

outcomes: Implement and explain a resilient one-dimensional component layout.

examples.title: Flex axes and sizing: concrete case
examples.situation: A column flex container uses justify-content for vertical distribution and align-items for horizontal cross-axis alignment.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A column flex container uses justify-content for vertical distribution and align-items for horizontal cross-axis alignment.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Name the current main and cross axes first; justify-content acts on the main axis, not a fixed physical direction.
examples.result: The result follows because Name the current main and cross axes first; justify-content acts on the main axis, not a fixed physical direction.

misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use a concrete CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling artifact to explain why this belief fails: justify-content always controls horizontal alignment.

analogy.example: A layered stage design can model Flex axes and sizing, where rules size, place, and paint existing content.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for Flex axes and sizing before using exact platform terms
limitations: Physical layers do not reproduce cascade algorithms, intrinsic sizing, writing modes, or user preferences.
